title: REST API Access Pattern Analysis (_audit)
spl:
(index=_audit action=rest) OR (index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd_access)
| stats count by user, uri_path, status
| where count > 500 OR status>=400
| sort -count
description: Automated token abuse and reconnaissance often appear as unusual REST paths or volumes.
value: Automated token abuse and reconnaissance often appear as unusual REST paths or volumes.
grandmaExplanation: Automated token abuse and reconnaissance often appear as unusual REST paths or volumes ‚Äî so you always trust the numbers you are looking at.
implementation: Baseline normal automation. Alert on new `uri_path` clusters or HTTP 401/403 bursts. Correlate with token ID if logged.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Audit trail / `splunkd_access`.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_audit` `action=rest` OR `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd_access`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Baseline normal automation. Alert on new `uri_path` clusters or HTTP 401/403 bursts. Correlate with token ID if logged.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
(index=_audit action=rest) OR (index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd_access)
| stats count by user, uri_path, status
| where count > 500 OR status>=400
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**REST API Access Pattern Analysis (_audit)** ‚Äî Automated token abuse and reconnaissance often appear as unusual REST paths or volumes.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_audit` `action=rest` OR `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd_access`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Audit trail / `splunkd_access`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _audit, _internal; **sourcetype**: splunkd_access. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=_audit, index=_internal, sourcetype=splunkd_access. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user, uri_path, status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where count > 500 OR status>=400` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (top REST paths), Line chart (4xx/5xx rate).
visualization: Table (top REST paths), Line chart (4xx/5xx rate).
